title: Decider per-run throughput audit: is decider getting enough 'umpf'? Are minor items left to rot until M1 safety-catches them? M1 should be a safety net, not a primary throughput mechanism.
scope_authority: Diagnostic investigation + design proposal (PROP-NNN). Operator reviews PROP before implementation. This DIRECTIVE may conclude that the current behavior is acceptable (operator wants the honest read), but the prior pattern of 'yeet pipeline' intervention suggests a real defect.
context: Empirical observation 2026-05-11T14:06Z: of 66 items currently in status='open', 37 are <2 days old, all curmudgeon-raised. Some have been sitting >24h (ISS-1925, ISS-1926 are 37h old as of inspection). These items have been through approximately 6 decider runs since arrival without being triaged.

Operator critique 2026-05-11T14:25Z: 'I will challenge you and say that for the stuff that's waited 2 days, it would wait forever and decider won't look at it except for the M1/M2 safety catch. And that's wrong behavior. It shouldn't need the safety catch for BAU.'

Historical precedent: an earlier operator-side intervention nicknamed the 'yeet pipeline' was needed to force decider to process work it wasn't prioritizing on its own. Decider has demonstrated a tendency to tap out — to do the 'interesting' work (curmudgeon digest top items, EXP integrations, major-severity issues) and leave minor/moderate items for later runs that never come.

The PROP-026 M1 stale-issue sweep was designed as a safety net for genuinely-stale items (age > 21d in BAU, > 7d in burndown). It was NOT designed to be the primary processing path for ordinary minor curmudgeon-raised items. The current empirical pattern (M1 routes 30 items/run, mostly minor/moderate curmudgeon-raised items that just sat in open status) indicates the safety net is doing primary-throughput work — which is the exact failure mode the design is supposed to prevent.

Prior framing in operator-Claude session (2026-05-11T14:15Z): operator-Claude told operator 'minor curmudgeon-raised items legitimately wait their turn... the May 9 fix was about integrity findings not falling through the cracks; it wasn't about throughput.' Operator rejected this framing: throughput IS the bug. Operator-Claude conceded the framing was wrong.
operator_intent: Tinker investigates whether decider is leaving boring work on the table. If yes, identify where in decider's run flow the tap-out happens and propose a fix. Goal state: in BAU, every minor curmudgeon-raised item should be triaged within ~12-24h of arrival. M1/M2 should fire occasionally on genuine edge cases (items operator-flagged for deferral, items blocked on external dependencies, etc.), not as the primary disposition path for ordinary throughput.

tasks.description: Reconstruct decider's actual per-run work output over the last 14 days. For each decider run, count: (a) curmudgeon reviews available in priority-queue and pending-digest, (b) curmudgeon reviews actually popped/processed, (c) open-issues count at start vs end, (d) closures by category (patch / route-to-analyst / route-to-curmudgeon / wontfix / pending-human / M1 / M2 / M3), (e) net change in open-bucket size. Show whether decider is closing the same number it's seeing, or whether items accumulate.
data_sources: monitor/decisions/closure-ledger.jsonl (PROP-026 audit trail with mechanism breakdown)
git log of data/ + monitor/decisions/ commits (commits-per-run as a coarse throughput proxy)
tasks.deliverable: A 14-day per-run table in the PROP context section: run_id | curmudgeon_reviews_available | reviews_processed | open_at_start | open_at_end | closures_by_mechanism. Trend chart in ASCII or summary stats.

tasks.description: Read decider.md + decider-curmudgeon.md + decider-intake.md + decider-patches-and-selfapply.md. Identify every place where decider can choose to stop short of full processing: priority-based caps, 'high-importance' filters, 'sufficient progress this run' heuristics, token/context budget exits, 'will be picked up next run' deferrals. Map each to a line/section reference. Hypothesize which one(s) are causing minor items to defer indefinitely.
candidates_to_audit: decider.md Priority 3 (curmudgeon digest): does decider process all pending-digest reviews or only top-N?
decider.md Priority 1b (analyst proposals): is there a queue cap?
decider.md Priority 5b (M1 sweep): K cap is intentional but Priority 3/4 caps may be the silent tap-out
decider's curmudgeon-digest read pattern: does it sort by severity / queue_id / age? Does it skip items that don't have clear actions?
Any 'I'll get to this next run' pattern in LLM behavior under high-context-load conditions
Whether decider's run-summary 'work done' framing implicitly rewards interesting work over boring work (analyst behavioral effect)
tasks.deliverable: A list of identified tap-out points in PROP context section, each with line reference + hypothesized impact on minor-item throughput.

tasks.description: Of items that eventually close, what fraction were closed via BAU pathways (Priority 1-4 normal processing) vs M1/M2/M3 safety nets? If safety-net mechanisms account for >20% of closures over the last 14 days, that's empirical evidence the safety nets are doing primary work. If <5%, the framing is correct and the safety nets are doing edge-case work as designed.
tasks.method: Parse closure-ledger.jsonl by closed_by_mechanism. Tally per-mechanism counts over 14d window. Compute ratios.
tasks.expected_finding_template: Over the last 14 days, decider closed X items total. Y were closed via BAU processing (action_taken in {patch, narrow-patch}). Z were closed via M1 sweep (action_taken in {patch, narrow-patch, wontfix, route-to-analyst} with closed_by_mechanism='M1'). W were closed via M2 (EXP-tied auto-close). The M1+M2 fraction is (Z+W)/X = ___ %. Interpretation: [if >20%]: safety nets are doing primary work, throughput bug confirmed. [if <5%]: safety nets are edge-case work, throughput is fine, operator critique should be re-examined.

tasks.description: If task_1-3 confirm the throughput bug, propose a structural fix. Design space outlined below.

design_options.option_A_priority_5b_inclusion_of_open_b: Extend Priority 5b (currently the M1 stale-issue sweep) to ALSO sweep the 'open' bucket each run regardless of age. So decider always processes K minor/moderate items per run from the open bucket before falling back to M1 candidates. Effectively: convert the safety-net K-cap into a primary-throughput K-cap. M1 becomes a true edge-case mechanism (only triggers when items >N_DAYS still in open after primary sweep).
design_options.option_B_curmudgeon_digest_completeness_: Add an explicit decider rule: process ALL pending curmudgeon-digest items each run, not just top-N. Items that can't be patched should be route-to-analyst or wontfix-with-rationale immediately, not deferred to a future run.
design_options.option_C_open_bucket_age_floor_alarm: Add a per-run alarm: if any item in status='open' is >24h old at run start, escalate to operator AND force-process before any non-critical work. Effectively: 24h becomes the soft SLO for open-bucket triage; failing the SLO is a tinker-flaggable defect, not a routine wait-for-next-run.
design_options.option_D_explicit_anti_tap_out_rule: Edit decider.md preamble with explicit anti-tap-out guidance: 'You MUST attempt triage on every item in pending-digest and every item in status=open before declaring run complete. Deferring to next run is allowed only for items requiring information you can fetch but choose not to (and that choice must be logged with rationale). Token budget is not a valid deferral reason — split the work across multiple commit cycles within the same run if needed.'
design_options.option_E_combination: Some mix of A+B+C+D depending on which tap-out points task_2 identifies.

design_questions_for_tinker: Q1: Which option (or combination) addresses the empirical pattern most cleanly?

Q2: How does the fix interact with existing per-run-token / per-run-context budget realities? If decider physically cannot process all 37 <2d items in one run, what's the right batching strategy? (Hint: maybe process all curmudgeon-digest items even if it means deferring an EXP integration to next run — flip the priority order.)
Q3: Will this fix increase decider's per-run cost meaningfully? If yes, name the magnitude estimate and confirm it's worth the throughput gain.
Q4: What's the right SLO target for minor-curmudgeon-raised items? 12h? 24h? 48h? Tinker proposes a number based on what's empirically achievable.

Q5: Does this fix interact with DIRECTIVE-005 (backlog-trend monitoring)? Tinker's backlog-trend metrics should include 'oldest item in status=open' as a leading indicator — if that creeps up, it's a precursor to M1-doing-primary-work pattern resuming.

tasks.description: Operator wants the honest read. Tinker MUST consider the possibility that the current behavior is acceptable, and document why if so. Specifically address: (a) Is per-run-token-budget a real and immovable constraint that forces some deferral? (b) Is the current K=30 M1 sweep actually doing primary work, or is it processing items that LEGITIMATELY waited (e.g., items blocked on a now-completed prerequisite)? (c) Is the 'yeet pipeline' historical episode actually evidence of the same defect, or a different pathology that's already been fixed? (d) Would the proposed fix introduce a worse failure mode (e.g., decider runs over time budget, runs fail to push, scheduled task gets disabled)?
tasks.deliverable: A 'counter-argument' section in the PROP. If after honest consideration tinker thinks the current behavior is acceptable, retire the DIRECTIVE with status='superseded' and rationale='throughput-is-fine, operator-critique-re-examined-and-disagreed-with'. Don't sugar-coat either direction — operator wants the right answer.

constraints: PROP-NNN authoring authority only. Tinker proposes; operator reviews; tinker or operator applies per PROP scope.

Respect existing PROP-026, PROP-027, and other PROP-026/027 implementation-completeness DIRECTIVEs (003, 004, 005). The new PROP should fit cleanly. In particular, this DIRECTIVE's fix should NOT reduce M1's safety-net value — M1 should still catch the genuine edge cases, just stop doing the primary-throughput work.
Output one PROP file under monitor/tinker/proposals/PROP-NNN-decider-throughput-anti-tap-out.json or similar.
If task_5 concludes throughput is fine, set this DIRECTIVE's status='superseded' with rationale rather than authoring a PROP that's just a justify-the-status-quo.
expected_output_files: monitor/tinker/proposals/PROP-NNN-decider-throughput-anti-tap-out.json (if task_4 chosen) OR DIRECTIVE status='superseded' (if task_5 concludes throughput is fine)
related_directives: DIRECTIVE-20260510-001-open-issues-backlog-clearance.json (PROP-026: deployed the M1/M2/M3 framework that this DIRECTIVE argues is doing more work than it should)
DIRECTIVE-20260510-002-m1-routing-matrix-with-prop025-integration.json (PROP-027: the routing matrix that defines M1 disposition paths)

DIRECTIVE-20260510-005-tinker-backlog-trend-monitoring.json (sibling — tinker's backlog-trend metrics should include 'oldest item in status=open' as the leading indicator for this DIRECTIVE's failure mode)
operator_notes_to_tinker: Operator framing 2026-05-11T14:25Z: 'I will challenge you and say that for the stuff that's waited 2 days, it would wait forever and decider won't look at it except for the M1/M2 safety catch. And that's wrong behavior. It shouldn't need the safety catch for BAU.' This is the load-bearing claim. Validate or refute it with data.

If you find the 'tap-out' is real and consistent across runs, the fix is structural (option D or E most likely). If you find it's intermittent or workload-dependent, the fix may be different (option C with an alarm threshold rather than a hard rule).

The 'yeet pipeline' historical episode is referenced informally. If you find concrete artifacts in monitor/ (HNOTEs, DIRECTIVES, change records, decider summaries) that document that prior intervention, cite them in the PROP context — they're the strongest evidence the failure mode is recurring rather than novel.

Be especially honest about task_5. Operator does not want a PROP that exists just to give the DIRECTIVE a deliverable. If throughput is genuinely fine, say so and supersede this DIRECTIVE. Sibling DIRECTIVES (003, 004, 005) are already in the queue; not every DIRECTIVE has to land a PROP.
